expanded_description: Piecewise defined functions represent a crucial advancement in mathematical modeling, allowing us to describe relationships that change behavior across different intervals of the domain. Unlike standard functions defined by a single expression (like $f(x) = x^2$), piecewise functions use multiple sub-functions, each applying to a specific subset of the domain. This structure mirrors real-world scenarios where rules change based on conditions: tax brackets impose different rates for different income levels, shipping costs vary by weight ranges, and phone plans charge differently based on usage tiers. In Pre-calculus, mastering piecewise functions develops precision in reading mathematical notation, interpreting conditional statements, and graphing discontinuous or multi-behavior curves. These functions bridge the gap between simple algebraic rules and complex real-world systems, introducing students to concepts like continuity, jumps, and defined boundaries. Understanding piecewise functions is essential for studying absolute value functions (which are inherently piecewise), step functions (like the greatest integer function), and later for defining functions in calculus using limits and integrals over specific intervals. Moreover, piecewise definitions appear in spline interpolation, computer programming (if-else logic), and engineering control systems. Mastery involves fluently switching between algebraic notation, graphical representation, and numerical evaluation, ensuring students can analyze functions that do not follow a single uniform rule across their entire domain.

core_concept.principle:
A piecewise defined function is a function defined by multiple sub-functions, where each sub-function applies to a specific interval of the domain.

Notation:
$f(x) = \begin{cases} \text{expression}_1 & \text{if } \text{condition}_1 \\ \text{expression}_2 & \text{if } \text{condition}_2 \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ \text{expression}_n & \text{if } \text{condition}_n \end{cases}$

Key Rules:
- Domain Partition: The conditions (intervals) must cover the entire domain of the function.
- Non-Overlapping: Typically, intervals do not overlap (except possibly at endpoints where one is inclusive and the other exclusive).
- Evaluation: To evaluate $f(a)$, determine which condition $a$ satisfies, then use the corresponding expression.
- Graphing: Graph each sub-function only over its specified interval. Use open circles for excluded endpoints ($<, >$) and closed circles for included endpoints ($\leq, \geq$).

Common Types:
- Absolute Value: $f(x) = |x| = \begin{cases} x & x \geq 0 \\ -x & x < 0 \end{cases}$
- Step Function: Constant values over intervals (e.g., greatest integer function).
- Combined Functions: Different algebraic rules (linear, quadratic, etc.) over different intervals.
core_concept.explanation: Think of a piecewise function as a set of instructions with 'if-then' conditions. Imagine a vending machine: if you put in less than $1, you get nothing; if you put in $1 to $2, you get a candy bar; if you put in more than $2, you get a soda. The output depends on which range your input falls into. In mathematics, this allows us to model situations where a single formula doesn't fit all cases. For example, a tax function might charge 10% on the first $50,000 and 20% on anything above that. To evaluate a piecewise function, you act like a traffic cop: first, check the input value against the conditions (the 'traffic lights'). Once you identify the correct interval (the 'green light'), you proceed with only that specific rule. Ignore the other rules—they don't apply to this input. When graphing, you draw each piece like a puzzle segment, ensuring you stop exactly where the condition says stop. Open circles mean 'stop just before this point'; closed circles mean 'stop including this point.' This precision is critical because it determines whether the graph is connected (continuous) or has breaks (discontinuous).

image.alt: Overview of piecewise function notation and structure
image.caption: Figure 2.7.1: Structure of a piecewise defined function showing multiple expressions and domain conditions.

sections.introduction: Piecewise notation uses a curly brace to group multiple function rules, each paired with a domain condition. Understanding how to read this notation is the first step toward evaluating and graphing these functions. The notation explicitly states which rule applies to which input values, requiring careful attention to inequality symbols and interval boundaries.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 2.7.2: Breaking down the components of piecewise function notation.

key_concept.definition:
Components of Piecewise Notation:
- Function Name: Typically $f(x)$, $g(x)$, etc.
- Curly Brace: Groups the sub-functions together.
- Sub-functions: The algebraic expressions (e.g., $2x$, $x^2$, $5$).
- Conditions: The inequalities defining where each sub-function applies (e.g., $x < 0$, $x \geq 0$).

Reading the Notation:
- Read vertically: Each row is a separate rule.
- Read horizontally: 'Use this expression IF this condition is true.'
- Check for gaps: Ensure the conditions cover all intended input values.
- Check for overlaps: Ensure no input value satisfies two conditions (unless the outputs match).

Standard Format:
Conditions are usually written in interval notation or inequality notation. Intervals are typically arranged from left to right (increasing x-values).
key_concept.context: Piecewise notation is common in computer science (switch statements, if-else blocks) and engineering (control systems with thresholds). In economics, marginal tax rates are defined piecewise. In physics, potential energy wells often have different definitions inside and outside a region. Understanding this notation prepares students for defining functions programmatically and analyzing systems with state changes. The clarity of piecewise notation allows complex behaviors to be described precisely without ambiguity about which rule applies where.

image.caption: Figure 2.7.3: Annotated example showing expressions, conditions, and boundaries.

worked_examples.title: Reading Piecewise Notation

image.caption: Figure 2.7.4: Interpreting the rules of a piecewise function.

worked_examples.problem: Given $f(x) = \begin{cases} 2x + 1 & \text{if } x < 0 \\ x^2 & \text{if } x \geq 0 \end{cases}$, describe the function in words.

step_by_step.calculation: For negative inputs, multiply by 2 and add 1.
For zero or positive inputs, square the input.

step_by_step.explanation: Boundary is at $x = 0$. First piece excludes 0, second includes 0.

step_by_step.calculation: Function uses a linear rule for negatives and a quadratic rule for non-negatives.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Each row defines a specific rule for a specific interval. Read conditions carefully to know which rule applies.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Domain from Notation

image.caption: Figure 2.7.5: Determining the overall domain from piecewise conditions.

worked_examples.problem: Find the domain of $g(x) = \begin{cases} 5 & \text{if } x \leq -2 \\ x + 1 & \text{if } -2 < x < 3 \\ 0 & \text{if } x \geq 3 \end{cases}$.

step_by_step.explanation: Do the intervals connect? $-2$ is covered by Piece 1. Values between -2 and 3 covered by Piece 2. $3$ is covered by Piece 3.
Take the union of all intervals.

worked_examples.concept_applied: The domain of a piecewise function is the union of the domains of all its pieces. Check for coverage and overlaps.

worked_examples.title: Writing Piecewise from Description

image.caption: Figure 2.7.6: Translating a verbal description into piecewise notation.

worked_examples.problem: Write a piecewise function for: 'Charge $10 per hour for the first 5 hours, and $15 per hour for every hour after 5.'

step_by_step.explanation: First 5 hours means $0 \leq x \leq 5$. Rate is $10/hr$.
After 5 hours means $x > 5$. Rate is $15/hr$ (assuming marginal rate).
step_by_step.calculation: $C(x) = 15x$ for $x > 5$ (Note: Real pricing might be more complex, but this models the prompt).

worked_examples.concept_applied: Translate verbal conditions into inequalities and associate them with the corresponding algebraic rules.

image.caption: Figure 2.7.7: Summary of reading and writing piecewise notation.

sections.introduction: Evaluating a piecewise function requires a two-step process: first, determine which interval the input value belongs to; second, substitute the input into the corresponding sub-function. This process emphasizes logical decision-making before algebraic computation. Students must resist the urge to use the wrong rule or multiple rules for a single input.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 2.7.8: The decision process for evaluating piecewise functions.

key_concept.definition:
Evaluation Process:
1. Identify Input: Note the value of $x$ to be evaluated.
2. Check Conditions: Test $x$ against each inequality condition.
3. Select Rule: Choose the single expression whose condition is true.
4. Substitute: Plug $x$ into the selected expression.
5. Calculate: Simplify to find the output.

Important Notes:
- Only ONE rule applies per input (functions must be single-valued).
- If $x$ falls on a boundary, check which inequality includes equality ($\leq$ or $\geq$).
- If $x$ is not in any interval, the function is undefined at that point.
- For variable inputs (e.g., $f(a)$), the result may depend on the value of $a$.
key_concept.context: Evaluation skills are critical for programming logic and algorithm design. In physics, evaluating piecewise potential functions determines forces in different regions. In economics, evaluating tax functions determines liability based on income brackets. The skill of selecting the correct rule based on conditions transfers to any scenario involving conditional logic. Understanding boundary evaluation is particularly important because it determines the function's value at transition points, which affects continuity and limits in calculus.

image.caption: Figure 2.7.9: Step-by-step decision tree for evaluating piecewise functions.

worked_examples.title: Evaluating at Specific Values

image.caption: Figure 2.7.10: Evaluating $f(x)$ for numerical inputs.

worked_examples.problem: Given $f(x) = \begin{cases} x - 1 & \text{if } x < 2 \\ 2x + 1 & \text{if } x \geq 2 \end{cases}$, find $f(0)$, $f(2)$, and $f(5)$.

step_by_step.explanation: Input $x = 2$. Check condition: $2 \geq 2$ is true (first is false).

worked_examples.concept_applied: Check the condition first. Use the rule associated with the true condition. Boundary values depend on inclusive inequalities.

worked_examples.title: Evaluating at Boundary Points

image.caption: Figure 2.7.11: Careful evaluation at interval transitions.

step_by_step.explanation: Input $x = 1$. This is the boundary value.

worked_examples.concept_applied: At boundaries, the inclusive inequality ($\leq$ or $\geq$) determines which rule to use. Only one rule can apply.

worked_examples.title: Evaluating with Variable Inputs

image.caption: Figure 2.7.12: Evaluating piecewise functions with algebraic inputs.

worked_examples.problem: Given $h(t) = \begin{cases} t^2 & \text{if } t < 0 \\ 2t & \text{if } t \geq 0 \end{cases}$, find $h(a)$ where $a$ is a negative number.

step_by_step.explanation: If $a$ were positive, we would use $2a$.
step_by_step.calculation: Result depends on the known sign of $a$.

worked_examples.concept_applied: When the input is a variable, use the given information about the variable to select the correct piece.

image.caption: Figure 2.7.13: Summary of evaluation strategies for piecewise functions.

sections.introduction: Graphing piecewise functions involves plotting each sub-function over its specific interval. The most critical aspect is handling the endpoints correctly: open circles indicate excluded points, while closed circles indicate included points. This visual precision reveals whether the function is continuous (connected) or discontinuous (broken) at the boundaries.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 2.7.14: Visual guide to open and closed circles on piecewise graphs.

key_concept.definition:
Graphing Steps:
1. Identify Pieces: List each sub-function and its interval.
2. Graph Lightly: Sketch each function across the whole plane lightly (as a guide).
3. Erase Outside: Erase portions outside the specified interval.
4. Mark Endpoints:
- Open Circle ($\circ$): For $<$ or $>$ (excluded).
- Closed Circle ($\bullet$): For $\leq$ or $\geq$ (included).
5. Darken Valid Segments: Trace over the valid portions boldly.

Continuity Check:
- If the open/closed circles meet at the same point, the graph is continuous there.
- If there is a vertical gap between endpoints, the graph has a jump discontinuity.

Vertical Line Test:
- Ensure no vertical line passes through both an open and closed circle at the same x-value (would violate function definition).
key_concept.context: Graphing piecewise functions is essential for visualizing real-world models like tax brackets (step graphs) or motion profiles (velocity changing over time). In calculus, the graph reveals where limits exist (continuous points) and where they don't (jumps). Open and closed circles are standard mathematical notation for interval boundaries; mastering them prevents errors in understanding domain and range. The skill of erasing invalid portions ensures the graph accurately represents the function's definition, preventing the common mistake of drawing the entire line instead of just the segment.

image.caption: Figure 2.7.15: Step-by-step visual process for graphing piecewise functions.

worked_examples.title: Graphing a Two-Piece Function

image.caption: Figure 2.7.16: Graphing $f(x) = \begin{cases} x+1 & x < 1 \\ 3-x & x \geq 1 \end{cases}$.

step_by_step.calculation: Draw line $y=x+1$. At $x=1$, $y=2$. Place OPEN circle at $(1, 2)$.
Draw line $y=3-x$. At $x=1$, $y=2$. Place CLOSED circle at $(1, 2)$.

step_by_step.explanation: Open circle at $(1, 2)$ and closed circle at $(1, 2)$ overlap.

worked_examples.final_answer: Graph consists of two rays meeting at $(1, 2)$.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Graph each piece on its domain. Use open/closed circles to indicate boundary inclusion. Check for continuity.

worked_examples.title: Graphing with a Jump Discontinuity

image.caption: Figure 2.7.17: Graphing a piecewise function with a break.

step_by_step.calculation: Draw horizontal line at $y=2$ ending at $x=0$. CLOSED circle at $(0, 2)$.
Draw line $y=x+3$ starting at $x=0$. At $x=0$, $y=3$. OPEN circle at $(0, 3)$.

step_by_step.explanation: Ensure no line connects the gap.
step_by_step.calculation: Horizontal ray left, diagonal ray right, gap at y-axis.

worked_examples.final_answer: Graph has a jump discontinuity at $x=0$.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Discontinuities occur when endpoint values do not match. Represent gaps clearly with open/closed circles.

worked_examples.title: Graphing a Quadratic and Linear Combination

image.caption: Figure 2.7.18: Combining different function types in piecewise graph.

step_by_step.calculation: Draw parabola for $x < 1$. At $x=1$, $y=1$. OPEN circle at $(1, 1)$.
Draw line for $x \geq 1$. At $x=1$, $y=1$. CLOSED circle at $(1, 1)$.

step_by_step.explanation: Combine parabola segment and line ray.
step_by_step.calculation: Smooth transition from curve to line at $(1, 1)$.

worked_examples.final_answer: Graph combines parabola left of 1 and line right of 1, continuous at 1.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Different function types (quadratic, linear) can be combined. Check meeting point for continuity.

image.caption: Figure 2.7.19: Summary of graphing techniques and endpoint notation.

sections.introduction: Analyzing piecewise functions requires determining the overall domain and range by combining the properties of each piece. Continuity is a key characteristic: a function is continuous if its graph has no breaks, jumps, or holes. For piecewise functions, continuity must be checked specifically at the boundary points where the rules change.

introduction_image.alt: Introduction to domain, range, and continuity
introduction_image.caption: Figure 2.7.20: Analyzing overall properties of piecewise functions.

key_concept.definition:
Domain: The union of all sub-domains (intervals).
- Check for gaps between intervals.
- Example: $(-\infty, 0] \cup (0, \infty) = (-\infty, \infty)$.

Range: The union of all sub-ranges (output values).
- Analyze the range of each piece over its restricted domain.
- Combine the resulting intervals.
- Example: Piece 1 range $[0, 4)$, Piece 2 range $[2, \infty)$ → Total Range $[0, \infty)$.

Continuity at a Point $c$:
A function is continuous at boundary $c$ if:
1. $f(c)$ is defined.
2. $\lim_{x \to c^-} f(x)$ exists (left limit).
3. $\lim_{x \to c^+} f(x)$ exists (right limit).
4. Left Limit = Right Limit = $f(c)$.

Types of Discontinuity:
- Jump: Left and right limits exist but are not equal.
- Hole: Limit exists but function is undefined or different.
- Infinite: One or both limits approach infinity.
key_concept.context: Domain and range analysis for piecewise functions is critical for understanding the full scope of the model. In engineering, the range tells you the possible output values (e.g., voltage limits). Continuity analysis is the precursor to calculus limits; if a function is discontinuous, it is not differentiable at that point. In economics, discontinuities might represent sudden price changes or tax jumps. Understanding how to combine ranges (union of intervals) prevents errors in stating the possible outputs of the system.

image.caption: Figure 2.7.21: Visualizing continuous vs. discontinuous boundaries.

worked_examples.title: Finding Domain and Range

image.caption: Figure 2.7.22: Determining domain and range from piecewise definition.

worked_examples.problem: Find the domain and range of $f(x) = \begin{cases} x + 2 & \text{if } x < 1 \\ 4 - x & \text{if } x \geq 1 \end{cases}$.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Domain is the union of input intervals. Range is the union of output intervals from each piece.

worked_examples.title: Checking Continuity at a Boundary

image.caption: Figure 2.7.23: Testing continuity at a transition point.

worked_examples.problem: Determine if $f(x) = \begin{cases} 2x & \text{if } x < 3 \\ x + 5 & \text{if } x \geq 3 \end{cases}$ is continuous at $x = 3$.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Continuity requires left limit = right limit = function value. A jump indicates discontinuity.

worked_examples.title: Finding Parameter for Continuity

image.caption: Figure 2.7.24: Solving for a constant to ensure continuity.

worked_examples.problem: Find $k$ so that $f(x) = \begin{cases} x^2 & \text{if } x \leq 2 \\ kx - 2 & \text{if } x > 2 \end{cases}$ is continuous.

step_by_step.explanation: For continuity at $x=2$, left limit must equal right limit.

worked_examples.concept_applied: To make a piecewise function continuous, equate the values of the pieces at the boundary point and solve for the unknown.

image.caption: Figure 2.7.25: Summary of analyzing piecewise function properties.

sections.section_title: 2.7.5 Common Errors and How to Avoid Them
sections.introduction: Piecewise functions introduce specific pitfalls related to condition checking, boundary handling, and graphing precision. Common errors include using the wrong rule, drawing graphs beyond their restricted domains, misplacing open and closed circles, and assuming continuity where none exists. Recognizing these errors helps students develop accuracy in evaluation and visualization.

classifications.value: Evaluating a piecewise function using an expression that does not match the input's interval.
characteristics: Ignoring the conditions and picking the first expression
Using the rule for $x > 0$ when input is negative
Confusing boundary values (e.g., using $<$ rule for $x =$ boundary)

image.caption: Figure 2.7.26: Error - selecting the incorrect sub-function.

examples.process: Error: For $f(x) = \begin{cases} x & x < 0 \\ x^2 & x \geq 0 \end{cases}$, calculating $f(-2)$ using $x^2$.

classifications.value: Incorrectly marking endpoints on the graph.
characteristics: Drawing both open and closed at same point incorrectly
classifications.behavior: Misrepresents the domain and continuity of the function.

image.caption: Figure 2.7.27: Error - incorrect endpoint notation.

examples.process: Error: Graphing $x < 2$ with a closed circle at 2.
examples.explanation: Correct: Strict inequality means open circle. Value at 2 is not included in this piece.
Correct: Inclusive inequality means closed circle. Value is defined.

classifications.value: Drawing the sub-function over its entire natural domain instead of the restricted interval.
characteristics: Drawing the entire line for a linear piece instead of just the segment
Drawing the full parabola instead of the restricted arc
Not erasing the 'guide' lines outside the interval
classifications.behavior: Creates an incorrect graph that violates the function definition.

image.caption: Figure 2.7.28: Error - extending graph beyond restricted interval.

examples.process: Error: Graphing $y = x + 1$ for $x < 0$ as a full line.
examples.explanation: Correct: Only draw the ray to the left of $x=0$. Erase the right side.

examples.process: Error: Drawing parabola $x^2$ for all $x$ when defined only for $x > 1$.
examples.explanation: Correct: Only draw the portion where $x > 1$.

classifications.value: Connecting pieces with a line even when there is a jump.
characteristics: Drawing a vertical line to connect jump discontinuities
Assuming pieces must meet because they are part of one function
classifications.behavior: Incorrectly implies the function is continuous.

image.caption: Figure 2.7.29: Error - falsely connecting discontinuous pieces.

examples.process: Error: Connecting $(0, 2)$ and $(0, 5)$ with a vertical line.
examples.explanation: Correct: Vertical lines violate the function definition. Leave the gap open.

examples.process: Error: Assuming $f(x)$ is continuous because it's one function name.
examples.explanation: Correct: Piecewise functions are often discontinuous at boundaries. Check limits.

classifications.value: Defining pieces with overlapping intervals that give different outputs.
characteristics: Violating the definition of a function (one output per input)
classifications.behavior: Creates a relation that is not a function.

image.caption: Figure 2.7.30: Error - overlapping intervals causing ambiguity.

examples.explanation: Correct: At $x=2$, both rules apply. $f(2)$ would be 2 and 3. Not a function. Change one to strict inequality.

classifications.value: Finding range by combining unrestricted ranges of sub-functions.
characteristics: Ignoring the domain restriction when finding piece range
Assuming range is $(-\infty, \infty)$ because one piece is linear
Not checking minimum/maximum values on restricted intervals
classifications.behavior: Incorrectly states the possible output values.

image.caption: Figure 2.7.31: Error - miscalculating range of pieces.

examples.process: Error: Range of constant $5$ for $x \in [0, 2]$ is $[0, 2]$.
examples.explanation: Correct: Output is always 5. Range is $\{5\}$.

worked_examples.title: Identifying and Correcting Piecewise Errors

image.caption: Figure 2.7.32: Finding and fixing common piecewise mistakes.

worked_examples.problem:
A student made the following errors. Identify and correct each:

a) Graphed $f(x) = \begin{cases} x & x < 1 \\ 2 & x \geq 1 \end{cases}$ with a closed circle at $(1, 1)$ for the first piece.
b) Evaluated $g(3)$ for $g(x) = \begin{cases} x^2 & x \leq 3 \\ x+1 & x > 3 \end{cases}$ using $x+1$.
c) Stated range of $h(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & x < 0 \\ 2 & x \geq 0 \end{cases}$ is $(-\infty, \infty)$.

step_by_step.explanation: Student used closed circle for strict inequality $x < 1$.
step_by_step.calculation: Correction: Use OPEN circle at $(1, 1)$. Closed circle belongs to second piece at $(1, 2)$.

step_by_step.explanation: Student used second rule for boundary value $x=3$.
step_by_step.calculation: Correction: $3 \leq 3$ is true. Use first rule. $g(3) = 3^2 = 9$.

step_by_step.explanation: Student assumed linear-like range for step function.
step_by_step.calculation: Correction: Outputs are only 1 and 2. Range is $\{1, 2\}$.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Check inequality symbols for circles, check boundary conditions for evaluation, and analyze actual outputs for range.

image.caption: Figure 2.7.33: Comprehensive guide to avoiding common piecewise errors.

sections.introduction: Apply all skills related to piecewise functions—reading notation, evaluating, graphing, determining domain/range, and checking continuity—to demonstrate mastery of this foundational topic. These problems integrate multiple concepts and prepare students for advanced modeling and calculus.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Practice Set

image.caption: Figure 2.7.34: Practice problems covering piecewise functions.

worked_examples.problem:
Complete the following problems:

1) Evaluate $f(0)$ for $f(x) = \begin{cases} 2x & x < 0 \\ 5 & x \geq 0 \end{cases}$.
2) Evaluate $f(-2)$ for same $f(x)$.
3) Graph $g(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & x < 1 \\ 3 & x \geq 1 \end{cases}$.
4) Domain of $h(x) = \begin{cases} x & x \neq 2 \\ 0 & x = 2 \end{cases}$.
5) Range of step function $y = \lfloor x \rfloor$ for $x \in [0, 3)$.
6) Is $f(x) = |x|$ piecewise?
7) Write $|x - 1|$ as piecewise.
8) Continuity of $\begin{cases} x & x < 1 \\ 2-x & x \geq 1 \end{cases}$ at $x=1$.
9) Find $k$ for continuity: $\begin{cases} kx & x < 2 \\ x+2 & x \geq 2 \end{cases}$.
10) Graph $y = \begin{cases} -x & x < 0 \\ x & x \geq 0 \end{cases}$.
11) Evaluate $f(1)$ for $\begin{cases} x^2 & x \leq 1 \\ 2x & x > 1 \end{cases}$.
12) Range of $\begin{cases} 0 & x < 0 \\ 1 & x \geq 0 \end{cases}$.
13) True/False: Piecewise functions can be continuous.
14) True/False: Each piece must be a different function type.
15) Domain of $\begin{cases} \sqrt{x} & x \geq 0 \\ -x & x < 0 \end{cases}$.
16) Graph $\begin{cases} 2 & x \leq 0 \\ 2 & x > 0 \end{cases}$.
17) Evaluate $f(0.5)$ for $\begin{cases} 1 & 0 \leq x < 1 \\ 2 & 1 \leq x < 2 \end{cases}$.
18) Write graph of line $y=x$ for $x \in [0, 5]$ as piecewise.
19) Continuity of $\begin{cases} 1/x & x \neq 0 \\ 0 & x = 0 \end{cases}$ at 0.
20) Range of $\begin{cases} x^2 & x < 0 \\ x & x \geq 0 \end{cases}$.

step_by_step.calculation: Horizontal line y=1 left, y=3 right.

worked_examples.final_answer: 1) 5; 2) -4; 3) Step graph; 4) R; 5) {0,1,2}; 6) Yes; 7) -(x-1)/x-1; 8) Continuous; 9) k=2; 10) V-shape; 11) 1; 12) {0,1}; 13) True; 14) False; 15) R; 16) Line y=2; 17) 1; 18) Restricted line; 19) Discontinuous; 20) [0, inf)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Comprehensive practice demonstrating mastery of piecewise evaluation, graphing, domain/range, and continuity.

image.caption: Figure 2.7.35: Detailed solutions to all practice problems.

summary: Piecewise defined functions use multiple sub-functions, each applying to a specific interval of the domain. Notation involves a curly brace grouping expressions with their corresponding conditions (inequalities). To evaluate, first identify which interval the input belongs to, then substitute into the corresponding rule. Graphing requires plotting each piece only over its restricted domain, using open circles for excluded endpoints ($<, >$) and closed circles for included endpoints ($\leq, \geq$). The overall domain is the union of all sub-domains; the range is the union of all sub-ranges. Continuity is checked at boundary points: if left limit = right limit = function value, the function is continuous there; otherwise, it has a discontinuity (often a jump). Common errors include using the wrong rule, misplacing open/closed circles, graphing beyond restricted domains, and assuming continuity. Piecewise functions model real-world scenarios with changing rules (tax brackets, shipping costs) and include important functions like absolute value and step functions. Mastery involves fluency in notation, evaluation, graphing, and analysis of domain, range, and continuity.

review_questions_image.alt: Comprehensive summary: piecewise notation, evaluation, graphing, domain/range, continuity
review_questions_image.caption: Figure 2.7.36: Complete reference for piecewise defined functions showing notation, evaluation steps, graphing techniques, and continuity analysis.
